---
title: "Milwaukee Bucks Business Analytics Hackathon"
description: "Third-place finish predicting premium seating pricing tiers at the Milwaukee Bucks Business Analytics Hackathon."
date: "2024-11-01"  # TODO: replace with actual hackathon date
categories: [machine-learning, classification, sports-analytics]
image: ../images/bucks.png
image-alt: "Thumbnail for Bucks hackathon project"
format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
---

## Overview

Third-place finish at the Milwaukee Bucks Business Analytics Hackathon. The challenge: build a machine learning model to predict the **premium seating pricing tier** for individual games at Fiserv Forum, given features about [TODO: list the kinds of features you got — opponent, day-of-week, time-of-season, historical demand, etc.].

[TODO: 1-2 more sentences on your team's approach — who was on the team, what made your submission stand out, what the time constraint was, etc.]

## Tech stack

[TODO: e.g., Python / scikit-learn / XGBoost / pandas — or R if that's what you used]

## Report

<iframe src="../pdfs/Bucks_Hackathon.pdf" width="100%" height="650px" style="border: 1px solid #ccc;"></iframe>

[Download report](../pdfs/Bucks_Hackathon.pdf)

## Presentation

<iframe src="../pdfs/Bucks_Presentation.pdf" width="100%" height="650px" style="border: 1px solid #ccc;"></iframe>

[Download presentation](../pdfs/Bucks_Presentation.pdf)

## Key findings

[TODO: 3-5 bullets on the headline result. Things worth covering:]

- **The winning model** — what algorithm, what test accuracy / F1 / whatever metric the judges scored on
- **Top features** — which inputs ended up actually driving the pricing tier (probably opponent quality + day-of-week + season timing, but use what your model said)
- **Anything counterintuitive** — features the team expected to matter that didn't, or vice versa
- **Why we placed 3rd, not 1st** — if you know what the top teams did differently, that's a great signal of self-awareness on a portfolio page
